返回本批次

前 Meta 员工因下载 3 万张 Facebook 私人照片而受到调查

Ex-Meta worker investigated for downloading 30k private Facebook photos

www.bbc.com 作者 1659447091 分数 67 评论 28 2026/04/08 06:20
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 11小时前劳拉·克雷斯科技记者盖蒂图片社一名前Meta员工涉嫌下载约3万张Facebook用户的私人图像，正在接受伦敦警察厅的调查。据信，这名住在伦敦的工程师设计了一个程序，能够在避免安全检查的同时访问该网站上的个人照片。Meta 发言人告诉 BBC，这一漏洞是在一年多前发现的，之后该公司表示立即解雇了这名可疑员工，并“将此事提交给执法部门”。大都会警察局发言人表示，一名 30 多岁的男子因涉嫌未经授权访问计算机材料而于 2025 年 11 月被捕。据新闻协会称，他已被保释，下次必须在 5 月份向警方报到。在接到美国联邦调查局的转介后，大都会警察局网络犯罪部门的官员正在调查这一事件。Meta 补充说，它已通知下载图像的 Facebook 用户，并已升级了安全措施此次泄露是 Facebook 母公司出现的最新安全问题。 2022 年 11 月，爱尔兰数据保护委员会 (DPC) 因数亿 Facebook 用户的个人详细信息在线发布而被爱尔兰数据保护委员会 (DPC) 罚款 2.65 亿欧元（2.28 亿英镑）。该公司还拥有 Instagram 和 WhatsApp，最近还因其应用程序的令人上瘾的设计而面临法律诉讼。这名名叫 Kaley 的女子获得了 600 万美元（450 万英镑）的赔偿。 Meta 和谷歌均表示不同意判决并打算上诉。

Original Summary

  1. 11 hours agoLaura CressTechnology reporterGetty ImagesA former Meta employee suspected of downloading around 30,000 private images of Facebook users is being investigated by the Metropolitan Police. The engineer, who lives in London, is believed to have designed a program to be able to access personal pictures on the site while avoiding security checks.A Meta spokesperson told the BBC the breach was discovered over a year ago, after which the firm said it immediately fired the suspected employee and "referred the matter to law enforcement". A spokesperson for the Metropolitan Police said a man in his 30s was arrested in November 2025 on suspicion of unauthorised access to computer material.He has since been released on bail, and must next report to police in May, according to the Press Association.The incident is being investigated by officers from the Metropolitan Police's Cybercrime Unit, after the force received a referral from the Federal Bureau of Investigation in the US.Meta added it had notified the Facebook users who had their images downloaded and had since upgraded its security systems.The breach is the latest security issue to emerge from the Facebook parent company. In November 2022 it was fined €265m (£228m) by the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) over a breach that saw the personal details of hundreds of millions of Facebook users published online.The company, which also owns Instagram and WhatsApp, has also faced recent legal battles over the addictive design of its apps. The woman, known as Kaley, was awarded $6m (£4.5m) in damages. Meta and Google said they disagreed with the verdict and intended to appeal.

评论（默认中文）

dietr1ch 2026/04/08 08:20
> 发现 Meta 在其内部系统中无意中存储了社交媒体用户的某些密码，且未加密，并对其处以 9100 万欧元（7500 万英镑）的罚款

搞什么？我认为在 2010 年，人们已经足够勤奋，甚至避免发送密码，而是在发送密码之前在本地对其进行哈希处理。

> found Meta to have inadvertently stored certain passwords of social media users on its internal systems without encryption, and fined it €91m (£75m)

WTF? I thought that on 2010 already people were diligent enough to avoid even sending the password and instead just hashed it locally before even sending it.

在 HN 中查看
varun_ch 2026/04/08 09:58
即使在今天，这也不是标准的。主要威胁是通过网络传输，https/TLS 可以解决这一问题，但显然这不会阻止错误跟踪或登录服务器以包含请求主体。

如果您在本地进行散列（不确定我是否见过任何大玩家这样做），您还需要在服务器端进行散列（否则散列基本上是数据库中的纯文本密码！）

也就是说，我不确定为什么公司不采用这种双重哈希方法。也许很复杂？我知道这可能会限制灵活性，因为某些服务希望能够在服务器上自动尝试大小写变化（例如大写锁定反转）。无论如何，到 2026 年，我们都应该使用万能钥匙（如果它们对最终用户来说不是那么令人困惑，而且是不可移植的）

That is not standard even today. The main threat is in transit over the network, which https/TLS solves, but obviously this won’t stop error traces or logging on the server from including request bodies.

If you do hash locally (not sure I’ve seen any big players do this), you also need to be hashing server side (or else the hash is basically a plain text password in the database!)

That said, I’m not sure why companies don’t adopt this double hashing approach. Complexity maybe? I know it could limit flexibility a little as some services like to be able to automatically attempt capitalization variations (eg. caps lock inverse) on the server. Anyways in 2026 we should all be using passkeys (if they weren’t so confusing to end-users, and so non-portable)

在 HN 中查看
f33d5173 2026/04/08 08:25
这从来都不是标准的。日志文件中的密码是一个常见问题，疯狂的是，您可能会因此被罚款 8 位数字。
That's never been standard. Passwords in log files is a common issue, crazy you can get fined 8 digits for it.
在 HN 中查看
burnt-resistor 2026/04/08 08:56
过去10年发生的事情非常令人怀疑。如果没有业务原因和管理层批准，几乎不可能访问图表上的任何内容。
Extremely doubtful to have occurred in the past 10 years. It's pretty much impossible to access anything on the graph without a business reason and managerial approval.
在 HN 中查看
xnx 2026/04/08 08:01
在 2007 年，这将是一个令人尴尬的安全漏洞。在 2024 年（？），这是卑鄙的。
This would've been an embarrassing security lapse in 2007. In 2024(?) it's despicable.
在 HN 中查看
cramsession 2026/04/08 07:43
有一次我和 Facebook 员工一起参加一个聚会，他们正在讲述如何监视谁访问了谁的个人资料的故事。他们以为那是 所以 有趣的是，他们可以“辨别”谁迷恋谁。我一回到家就删除了我的帐户。无良公司。
I was at a party once with Facebook employees and they were telling stories about how they would spy on who visited who's profiles. They thought it was so funny, they could "tell" who had a crush on who. I deleted my account as soon as I got home. Vile company.
在 HN 中查看
phyrex 2026/04/08 08:14
那一定是很久以前的事了。如今有很多保障措施，这也是导致你立即被解雇的原因之一。
That must have been a long time ago. Nowadays there are a lot of safeguards and that's one of the things that gets you fired right away.
在 HN 中查看
jnsaff2 2026/04/08 08:20
如今，当您访问某人的个人资料时，您会出现在他们建议的朋友列表中。令人毛骨悚然或可爱，故意泄露信息。
Nowadays when you visit someones profile you show up on their suggested friend list. Creepy or cute, a deliberate information leak.
在 HN 中查看
em-bee 2026/04/08 08:50
在别人不知情的情况下查看某人的个人资料并不令人毛骨悚然？
viewing someones profile without them knowing is not creepy?
在 HN 中查看
hackable_sand 2026/04/08 09:00
太可怕了，他们就是这么说的。
It is creepy, that's what they're saying.
在 HN 中查看
em-bee 2026/04/08 09:20
我的理解是“出现在他们建议的朋友列表上是令人毛骨悚然的，这是信息泄露”。我的理解是，当有人访问他们的个人资料时，他们不愿意显示。这就是我认为令人毛骨悚然的地方。
what i understood is that "showing up on their suggested friends list is creepy, and it's an information leak". the way i read that is that they would prefer not to show when someone visited their profile. and that's what i consider creepy.
在 HN 中查看
kakacik 2026/04/08 08:49
在过去的十多年里，每当@fb 发生类似的事情时，我都会在这里阅读同样的声明。每一个。单身的。时间。

对该公司0信任，对其员工0信任。

I keep reading same statements here for past 10+ years, every time some similar fuckup @fb happens. Every. Single. Time.

0 trust in that company, 0 trust in its employees.

在 HN 中查看
ryandrake 2026/04/08 07:51
不会让我感到惊讶。当你提到 Zucc 的引言时，每个人都紧握着自己的珍珠，按下投反对票的按钮，但真的有任何证据表明公司文化已经从“他们信任我 - 笨蛋”中走向成熟了吗？
Wouldn't surprise me. Everyone clutches their pearls and hits the downvote button as soon as you mention the Zucc quote, but has there really been any evidence that the company culture has matured away from "They Trust Me - Dumb fucks"?

DANmode 2026/04/08 08:06

Wouldn’t it be nice if the scope of what you witnessed was limited to that one company…

actionfromafar 2026/04/08 08:11

DANmode 2026/04/08 08:13

但重点是： Facebook 吸引了这些员工，而不是培养他们。

Google, since you asked.

But the point is: Facebook attracts these employees, it doesn’t breed them.

livinglist 2026/04/08 08:04
他们是否能够在没有任何踪迹的情况下随时查看任何用户的这些数据？
Are they able to see these data of whichever user whenever they want with no trails at all??
在 HN 中查看
cramsession 2026/04/08 08:08
听起来确实是这样，或者说没有人关心这些小径，因为他们认为这太搞笑了。
It certainly sounded like it, or that no one cared about the trails since they thought it was so hilarious.
在 HN 中查看
burnt-resistor 2026/04/08 08:59
绝对不是。我不是 Zucc 的朋友，但该图受到权限系统的保护，如果没有提出请求（包括合法的商业原因、在有限的时间和范围内以及管理层批准），该系统不会向员工显示几乎任何内容。
Absolutely not. I'm no friend of Zucc, but the graph is protected by a permission system that won't show almost anything for employees without a making a request including legitimate business reason, for a limited time and scope, and managerial approval.
在 HN 中查看

cramsession 2026/04/08 07:54
这不仅仅是一个人，是的，他们能够访问这些信息并形成一种间谍文化（并开玩笑），这是可耻的。他们还表示，他们可以判断出某人观看每张图片的时间。整个公司基本上都是变态的间谍软件，如果你知道它是如何以及为何构思的，那么这绝对是有道理的。
It was more than one person and yes, it's vile that they had access to this information and a culture of spying (and joking about it). They also said they could tell how long someone was looking at each image. The whole company is basically perverted spyware, which absolutely makes sense if you know how and why it was conceived.
在 HN 中查看
hyperhello 2026/04/08 07:57

You could make that claim about all of public society in some way. Why go anywhere, unless it’s to be spied on and spy on others.
